summary: Ancient Greek philosopher, founder of the Lyceum and Peripatetic School, student of Plato, teacher of Alexander the Great. Known as 'the First Teacher' in Islamic philosophy. Author of the Metaphysics, Nicomachean Ethics, Physics, Politics, Poetics, De Anima, and the Organon. Associated with hylomorphism, four causes, the unmoved mover, eudaimonia, virtue ethics, and the doctrine of the mean.

works.name: Metaphysics and 'Being qua Being'
works.description: The Metaphysics is Aristotle's most frequently clued single work. It concerns 'being qua being' and 'first philosophy.' Key clues include the unmoved mover (a 'thought thinking about thinking'), the four causes (formal, efficient, material, final), the concepts of potentiality and actuality, and the opening line 'All men by nature desire to know.' Book Zeta asks 'what is substance?' Book Twelve discusses 47 or 55 gods needed for heavenly motions. The Metaphysics was enormously influential in Islamic philosophy: Avicenna read it 40 times, al-Farabi wrote a commentary on its 'Purposes,' al-Kindi used its infinite regress argument, and Averroes wrote the famous 'Long Commentary.'

clues.clue: Concerns 'being qua being' / 'first philosophy'; unmoved mover as 'thought thinking about thinking'
examples: a study of 'being qua being' by Aristotle
a 'thought thinking about thinking' which turns potentialities to actualities

examples: Formal, efficient, material, and final causes are discussed in this book

examples: the view that there is a 'unity of the intellect,' which a thinker termed 'monopsychicism,' is introduced in Averroes's Long Commentary

clues.clue: Opening: 'All men by nature desire to know'
examples: Its opening paragraph claims that we delight in senses like vision since 'All men by nature desire to know'

works.name: Nicomachean Ethics and Virtue Ethics
works.description: The Nicomachean Ethics, named for Aristotle's son, is the founding text of virtue ethics. Eudaimonia (happiness/flourishing) is identified as the highest good. The Doctrine of the Mean holds each virtue lies between two extremes (e.g., courage between cowardice and rashness). Phronesis (practical wisdom) helps cultivate hexis (dispositions). The 'function argument' identifies the 'rational principle' as uniquely human. Book IX divides friendships into utility, pleasure, and goodness. Megalopsuchia ('great-souledness') includes a slow gait and deep voice. The work endorses Socrates's claim that no one knowingly chooses evil (akrasia). Alasdair MacIntyre's After Virtue and Anscombe's 'Modern Moral Philosophy' revived virtue ethics based on this work.

clues.clue: Eudaimonia (happiness/flourishing) as the highest good
examples: Happiness, or eudaimonia, is identified as the highest good

clues.clue: Doctrine of the Mean: each virtue is the average of two extremes
examples: each virtue is the average of two extremes in its Doctrine of the Mean

clues.clue: Phronesis (practical wisdom) helps cultivate hexis (dispositions)
examples: 'practical wisdom,' or phronesis, helps one cultivate dispositions, or hexis

clues.clue: Function argument: rational principle makes humans unique (vs. plants with life, animals with perception)
examples: this book considers life, which is shared by plants, and perception, which is shared by animals, before deciding that the 'rational principle' makes humans unique

clues.clue: Book IX: friendships divided into utility, pleasure, and goodness of character
examples: the ninth section of this book divides friendships into those based on utility, pleasure, and goodness of character

clues.clue: Megalopsuchia: slow gait, deep voice; W.D. Ross translated as 'proud man'
examples: describes a slow gait and a deep voice as attributes of the 'great-souled' person

examples: Alasdair MacIntyre's After Virtue helped spur the recent revival of virtue ethics

examples: adds the qualifier 'in a full life' to a central definition because 'one swallow does not make a summer'

works.name: Physics and the Four Causes
works.description: Aristotle's Physics discusses change, motion, and nature. It introduces the four causes using the example of an architect designing a house (or a bronze statue). Time and space are 'continuous' because indefinitely divisible. The work refutes Zeno's paradoxes and argues that the impossibility of actual infinities means change must have begun with a Prime Mover. Aristotle rejected actual infinites while allowing potential infinites by division. Whitehead's process philosophy updates the four causes.

clues.clue: Four causes illustrated with architect/house or bronze statue example
examples: uses the example of an architect designing a house to illustrate his four causes
introduces a crucial set of conceptual distinctions with the example of a bronze statue

clues.clue: Refutes Zeno's paradoxes; time and space are 'continuous' (indefinitely divisible)
examples: time and space are characteristically 'continuous' because they can be indefinitely divided in a section refuting Zeno's paradoxes

clues.clue: Actual infinities impossible; leads to Prime Mover argument
examples: the impossibility of actual infinities means that change in the world must have begun with a Prime Mover

clues.clue: Hylomorphism: substances are complexes of form and matter
examples: substances are predicative complexes of form and matter, his theory of hylomorphism

works.name: Pseudo-Aristotelian Works and Islamic Reception
works.description: A major clue cluster concerns works falsely attributed to Aristotle. The Theology of Aristotle is actually a paraphrase of Plotinus's Enneads. The Secretum Secretorum (framed as a letter to Alexander the Great) influenced Roger Bacon. The Book of Causes was shown by Aquinas to derive from Proclus's Elements of Theology. 'Aristotle's Masterpiece' was a popular sex manual. The 'recovery' of Aristotle's works in medieval Europe resulted in many false attributions. Aristotle was called 'the First Teacher'; al-Farabi was 'the Second Teacher'; Averroes was 'the Commentator.' Boethius's translation of Porphyry's Isagoge was an introduction to Aristotle's Categories.

clues.clue: Theology of Aristotle: actually paraphrase of Plotinus's Enneads
examples: a book named for this man's theology which was traditionally attributed to this man is an Arabic paraphrase of parts of Plotinus's Enneads

clues.clue: Secretum Secretorum: letter to Alexander the Great; influenced Roger Bacon
examples: Roger Bacon published an edited version of the encyclopedic treatise Secretum Secretorum, attributing it to this man

clues.clue: Book of Causes: Aquinas showed it derives from Proclus's Elements of Theology
examples: Thomas Aquinas showed that a text with this property derived the maxim 'all is in all' from Proclus' Elements

clues.clue: 'Aristotle's Masterpiece': popular sex manual until 19th century
examples: the most widely reprinted medical book in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries was a sex manual

clues.clue: Called 'the First Teacher'; al-Farabi 'the Second Teacher'; Averroes 'the Commentator'
examples: In the Classical Arabic world, he was called 'the First Teacher'

examples: Porphyry's Isagoge was an introduction to one of this philosopher's works

works.name: Politics
works.description: The Politics argues that 'man is by nature a political animal.' It uses the analogy of a hand detached from the body to show humans are naturally part of a polis. The work controversially claims slaves lack the deliberative element while women possess it but it 'lacks authority.' It includes a sixfold classification of constitutions. Joan Tronto and Hannah Arendt engage with its distinction between public (polis) and private (oikos). Aristotle argues citizenship should be extended to those who hold public office.

clues.clue: 'Man is by nature a political animal'
examples: this book famously states that 'man is by nature' an animal concerned with this book's subject

clues.clue: Analogy of hand detached from body; Sophocles's Philoctetes as 'corpse among the living'
examples: uses the analogy of a hand being detached from its body

clues.clue: Slaves lack deliberative element; women possess it but it 'lacks authority'
examples: argues that slaves lack the deliberative element, while women possess it but it 'lacks authority'

clues.clue: Sixfold classification of constitutions (three true, three perverted)
examples: includes a sixfold classification of constitutions, three of which are true constitutions

works.name: Poetics and De Anima
works.description: The Poetics concerns tragedy and introduces mimesis (imitation) and catharsis (purification/purgation). Poets are generally better at desis than lusis. A suffering animal illustrates 'tragic pleasure.' De Anima divides mental capacities into nutritive, perceptual/sensitive, and intellectual/rational souls. Book III introduces active vs. passive intellect (nous poietikos vs. nous pathetikos), which became a major debate in medieval Arabic philosophy. The concept of tabula rasa originates here.

clues.clue: Poetics: mimesis (imitation) and catharsis; primarily about tragedy
examples: argues that this discipline originated from mimesis, or 'imitation,' and introduced the concept of 'catharsis'

clues.clue: De Anima: nutritive, sensitive, rational souls; active vs. passive intellect
examples: divided mental capacities into the nutritive, perceptual, and intellectual souls
the difference between nous poietikos and nous pathetikos

clues.clue: Tabula rasa originates in De Anima
examples: this Aristotelian treatise from which the term tabula rasa originates

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: General identification: student of Plato, founder of the Lyceum, teacher of Alexander the Great. Works cited with Bekker numbers; standard English edition edited by Jonathan Barnes. The Organon collects his logical works. Introduced the syllogism. His 'future contingents' problem uses the sea battle example (Elizabeth Anscombe wrote 'Aristotle and the Sea Battle'). James of Venice's translation demarcates logica nova vs. logica vetus. Aquinas reconciled Aristotle with Christianity. Duns Scotus and Aquinas influenced by hylomorphism and four causes. William of Ockham reduced Aristotle's ten categories to substance and quality.

clues.clue: Student of Plato; founder of the Lyceum / Peripatetic School; teacher of Alexander the Great
examples: this founder of the Lyceum's Peripatetic School, the most famous student of Plato

clues.clue: Bekker numbers used to cite his works; Jonathan Barnes edited standard English edition
examples: His works are cited with Bekker numbers, and their standard English edition was edited by Julian Barnes's brother, Jonathan

clues.clue: Future contingents: 'Tomorrow there will be a sea battle' (neither true nor false)
examples: used examples like 'Tomorrow there will be a sea battle' to argue that future contingents are neither true nor false

clues.clue: Aquinas reconciled Aristotle with Christianity; referred to him as 'The Philosopher'
examples: Aquinas's Summa Theologica refers to this thinker as 'The Philosopher'

clues.clue: Ockham reduced ten categories to substance and quality to reconcile with transubstantiation
examples: an attempt to reconcile this philosopher's thought with transubstantiation led to the claim that only 'substance' and 'quality' were real

clues.clue: History of the Animals: first to arrange organisms on a 'Great Chain of Being'
examples: In his History of the Animals, this thinker was the first to arrange organisms on a 'Great Chain of Being'

comprehensive_summary:
Aristotle is one of the most broadly clued philosophers in quizbowl, with clues spanning his own works, pseudo-Aristotelian texts, and his massive influence on Islamic and medieval European philosophy. The Metaphysics is the most frequently clued individual work, concerning 'being qua being' with key concepts including the unmoved mover (a 'thought thinking about thinking'), the four causes, and potentiality and actuality. Its reception in the Islamic world is a major clue cluster: Avicenna famously read it 40 times, al-Farabi was called 'the Second Teacher' for his commentary, Averroes's Long Commentary introduced 'monopsychicism,' and al-Kindi used its infinite regress argument.

The Nicomachean Ethics, named for Aristotle's son, is the founding text of virtue ethics. It identifies eudaimonia as the highest good and proposes the Doctrine of the Mean. Phronesis and the 'function argument' are frequently clued. The modern revival of virtue ethics by MacIntyre (After Virtue) and Anscombe ('Modern Moral Philosophy') draws heavily on this work.

The Physics introduces the four causes (often illustrated with an architect/house or bronze statue), refutes Zeno's paradoxes, and argues for the Prime Mover. Hylomorphism (form and matter) is central. The Politics is known for 'man is by nature a political animal,' its controversial views on slaves and women, and its sixfold classification of constitutions.

Pseudo-Aristotelian works form a distinct clue cluster. The Theology of Aristotle is actually Plotinus. The Secretum Secretorum influenced Roger Bacon. The Book of Causes derives from Proclus. 'Aristotle's Masterpiece' was a sex manual. Porphyry's Isagoge, an introduction to the Categories, was translated by Boethius and became a medieval logic standard.

The Poetics introduces mimesis and catharsis. De Anima divides the soul into nutritive, sensitive, and rational components, with the active/passive intellect distinction becoming central to medieval Arabic debates. Aristotle introduced the syllogism, the problem of future contingents (sea battle example), and his works are cited using Bekker numbers.
recursive_suggestions: Nicomachean Ethics - heavily clued as its own answerline
Poetics (Aristotle) - separate topic with catharsis and mimesis

Philosopher: Averroes's Long Commentary on this thinker's Metaphysics introduced the 'unity of the intellect' or 'monopsychicism'
Work: the 'function argument' considers life (shared with plants) and perception (shared with animals) before identifying the 'rational principle' as uniquely human
Work: phronesis (practical wisdom) helps cultivate hexis (dispositions) that lie at a 'mean' between excess and deficiency
Work: describes a slow gait and deep voice as attributes of megalopsuchia, or 'great-souledness,' which W.D. Ross translated as the 'proud man'
Work: adds 'in a full life' because 'one swallow does not make a summer' to its central definition of eudaimonia
Philosopher: used the example of 'Tomorrow there will be a sea battle' to argue that future contingents are neither true nor false
Philosopher: the Theology attributed to this thinker is actually an Arabic paraphrase of Plotinus's Enneads
Philosopher: the Secretum Secretorum, framed as a letter from the 'First Teacher' to Alexander the Great, greatly influenced Roger Bacon
Philosopher: Aquinas showed that the Book of Causes attributed to this thinker actually derives from Proclus's Elements of Theology
Work: argues that 'man is by nature a political animal' and uses the analogy of a hand detached from its body
Work: argues that poetry originated from mimesis ('imitation') and introduced the concept of catharsis ('purification')
Work: divides mental capacities into nutritive, sensitive, and rational souls; introduces active vs. passive intellect (nous poietikos vs. nous pathetikos)
Philosopher: Porphyry's Isagoge is an introduction to this thinker's Categories; Boethius translated it, contributing to a medieval rediscovery
